source_paper_title: In This Apportionment Lottery, the House Always Wins

Less satisfying is that these “divisor criteria” do not satisfy any bounds such as \(t \le d(t) \le t+1\), which makes it likely that solutions substantially deviate from proportionality ex post. An interesting question for future work is whether Theorem 2 can be strengthened to additionally satisfy lower quota ("\(F_i(p,h) \ge \lfloor q_i\rfloor\)") or upper quota ("\(F_i(p,h) \le \lceil q_i\rceil\)").
title: Add one-sided quota bounds to population-monotone ex ante proportional apportionment methods
background_latex:
Fix a set of states (or parties) \(N=\{1,\dots,n\}\) with \(n\ge 2\). A population profile is a vector \(p\in \mathbb{N}_+^n\), where \(p_i\) is the population of state \(i\). For a house size \(h\in \mathbb{N}_+\), the standard (fractional) quota of state \(i\) is
\[
q_i(p,h) \;:=\; h\,\frac{p_i}{\sum_{j\in N} p_j}.
\]
An apportionment is an integer vector \(a\in \mathbb{N}^n\) with \(\sum_{i\in N} a_i=h\).

A randomized apportionment method is a random procedure \(F\) that, given \((p,h)\), outputs a random apportionment \(F(p,h)\in \mathbb{N}^n\) with total \(h\). The method satisfies:

1) Ex ante proportionality if for every \((p,h)\) and each \(i\in N\),
\[
\mathbb{E}[F_i(p,h)] = q_i(p,h).
\]

2) Population monotonicity ex post ("universal" population monotonicity) if for every outcome of the internal randomness (equivalently, for every deterministic apportionment rule \(f\) in the support of \(F\)), the following holds: there is no pair of states \(i\ne j\) and no two inputs \((p,h)\), \((p',h')\) with \(p'_i\ge p_i\) and \(p'_j\le p_j\) such that simultaneously \(f_i(p',h')< f_i(p,h)\) and \(f_j(p',h')> f_j(p,h)\). (Intuitively, no "population paradox": a state whose population weakly increases should not lose seats while another whose population weakly decreases gains seats.)

Define one-sided quota constraints for an apportionment method \(F\):

Lower quota: for every \((p,h)\) and \(i\), \(F_i(p,h)\ge \lfloor q_i(p,h)\rfloor\) holds almost surely.

Upper quota: for every \((p,h)\) and \(i\), \(F_i(p,h)\le \lceil q_i(p,h)\rceil\) holds almost surely.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether there exists a randomized apportionment method \(F\) that satisfies population monotonicity and ex ante proportionality and, in addition, satisfies at least one of the following one-sided quota properties:

1) Lower quota: \(F_i(p,h)\ge \lfloor q_i(p,h)\rfloor\) almost surely for all \((p,h)\) and \(i\in N\).

2) Upper quota: \(F_i(p,h)\le \lceil q_i(p,h)\rceil\) almost surely for all \((p,h)\) and \(i\in N\).
latex:
Fix a set of states (or parties) \(N=\{1,\dots,n\}\) with \(n\ge 2\). A population profile is a vector \(p\in \mathbb{N}_+^n\), where \(p_i\) is the population of state \(i\). For a house size \(h\in \mathbb{N}_+\), the standard (fractional) quota of state \(i\) is
\[
q_i(p,h) \;:=\; h\,\frac{p_i}{\sum_{j\in N} p_j}.
\]
An apportionment is an integer vector \(a\in \mathbb{N}^n\) with \(\sum_{i\in N} a_i=h\).

A randomized apportionment method is a random procedure \(F\) that, given \((p,h)\), outputs a random apportionment \(F(p,h)\in \mathbb{N}^n\) with total \(h\). The method satisfies:

1) Ex ante proportionality if for every \((p,h)\) and each \(i\in N\),
\[
\mathbb{E}[F_i(p,h)] = q_i(p,h).
\]

2) Population monotonicity ex post ("universal" population monotonicity) if for every outcome of the internal randomness (equivalently, for every deterministic apportionment rule \(f\) in the support of \(F\)), the following holds: there is no pair of states \(i\ne j\) and no two inputs \((p,h)\), \((p',h')\) with \(p'_i\ge p_i\) and \(p'_j\le p_j\) such that simultaneously \(f_i(p',h')< f_i(p,h)\) and \(f_j(p',h')> f_j(p,h)\). (Intuitively, no "population paradox": a state whose population weakly increases should not lose seats while another whose population weakly decreases gains seats.)

Define one-sided quota constraints for an apportionment method \(F\):

Lower quota: for every \((p,h)\) and \(i\), \(F_i(p,h)\ge \lfloor q_i(p,h)\rfloor\) holds almost surely.

Upper quota: for every \((p,h)\) and \(i\), \(F_i(p,h)\le \lceil q_i(p,h)\rceil\) holds almost surely.

Determine whether there exists a randomized apportionment method \(F\) that satisfies population monotonicity and ex ante proportionality and, in addition, satisfies at least one of the following one-sided quota properties:

1) Lower quota: \(F_i(p,h)\ge \lfloor q_i(p,h)\rfloor\) almost surely for all \((p,h)\) and \(i\in N\).

2) Upper quota: \(F_i(p,h)\le \lceil q_i(p,h)\rceil\) almost surely for all \((p,h)\) and \(i\in N\).
location_in_paper: Page 8 (PDF page 10), end of Section 3.2 (immediately after Theorem 2 and discussion)
open_problem_quote: Less satisfying is that these “divisor criteria” do not satisfy any bounds such as \(t \le d(t) \le t+1\), which makes it likely that solutions substantially deviate from proportionality ex post. An interesting question for future work is whether Theorem 2 can be strengthened to additionally satisfy lower quota ("\(F_i(p,h) \ge \lfloor q_i\rfloor\)") or upper quota ("\(F_i(p,h) \le \lceil q_i\rceil\)").
counterexample_suitability_rationale: If the strengthened property is impossible, one could search over small \(n\), small \(h\), and small integer population profiles \(p\) to falsify candidate constructions or to reveal structural obstructions, because the constraints (monotonicity and one-sided quota) are finitely checkable on bounded domains. However, the space of randomized methods is infinite-dimensional, so fully automated counterexample searches would likely need to restrict to structured families (e.g., lottery over a finite set of deterministic rules), limiting conclusiveness.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can represent candidate methods programmatically (e.g., parameterized randomized seat-allocation rules) and evaluate them on a large suite of randomly generated profiles to estimate violations of population monotonicity, ex ante proportionality, and one-sided quota; this yields an automatic fitness signal. The challenge is that passing tests on finite instances does not certify universal validity, so the loop is better suited to discovering promising constructions or likely impossibility patterns than producing a definitive resolution.

verification.notice: Extracted from the source paper and passed an automated fidelity audit; not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Gölz–Peters–Procaccia, 2022) frames randomized apportionment as a way to achieve exact proportionality in expectation while enforcing strong ex-post axioms by requiring them to hold for every deterministic rule in the support (universal/ex-post). It proves a sharp baseline obstruction: quota (two-sided, i.e., always in \(\{\lfloor q_i\rfloor,\lceil q_i\rceil\}\)) is incompatible with population monotonicity even without neutrality/order-preservation assumptions (Theorem 1). This makes the present open problem natural: can one weaken quota to a one-sided bound (either \(F_i\ge \lfloor q_i\rfloor\) a.s. or \(F_i\le \lceil q_i\rceil\) a.s.) while keeping universal population monotonicity and ex-ante proportionality?

Forward-citing work has not yet resolved this existence question. "New combinatorial insights for monotone apportionment" studies the most obvious candidate family for universal population monotonicity—stationary divisor methods—and shows that, instance by instance, there are parameter ranges where a fixed divisor method satisfies upper quota and (separately) ranges where it satisfies lower quota. However, it also gives a strong barrier to the mixing approach suggested by the source paper’s methodology: randomizing over stationary divisor methods can still yield expected allocations far from quota (deviation nearly linear in house size), indicating that universal population monotonicity plus ex-ante proportionality is not easily obtained by simply mixing population-monotone divisor rules while hoping for quota-like bounds.

On the rounding/lottery side, "Monotone randomized apportionment" develops monotonicity notions for randomized rounding that control probabilities of selecting particular coalitions (e.g., selection monotonicity) and identifies Sampford sampling as satisfying a strong such axiom, while also proving impossibilities for even stronger threshold-style monotonicity notions under full-support quota and ex-ante proportionality. Although these axioms differ from universal population monotonicity across population profiles, the techniques and impossibility patterns suggest that adding almost-sure quota constraints tends to clash with strong monotonicity requirements. Related work in adjacent domains (e.g., "Candidate monotonicity and proportionality for lotteries and non-resolute rules" and "Affirmative Action's Cumulative Fractional Assignments") shows that dependent-rounding decompositions can simultaneously implement exact marginals and enforce hard ex-post bounds in structured settings, but these results do not address population-paradox monotonicity for apportionment rules.

Overall, the problem remains open: no forward citation provides either a construction of a randomized apportionment method that is universally population-monotone, ex-ante proportional, and satisfies an almost-sure one-sided quota bound, nor an impossibility theorem ruling out such a method. The most promising directions appear to be (i) strengthening the source paper’s population-monotone ex-ante proportional construction (based on state-specific Poisson/divisor criteria) by controlling its ex-post deviations to meet one-sided quota, or (ii) proving a new impossibility that extends Theorem 1 from two-sided quota to one-sided quota under universal population monotonicity, potentially using the combinatorial counterexample style of Theorem 1 together with constraints implied by ex-ante proportionality.

sources.title: Monotone randomized apportionment
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper introduces new monotonicity axioms for randomized apportionment/rounding and proves that Sampford sampling (Sampford rounding) satisfies their key axiom of selection monotonicity: if residues p\u00b4\u00b4 and \u00cp\u00b4\u00b4\u2032\u00b4\u00b4 sum to the same integer \(k\) and \(p'_i\ge p_i\) for all \(i\in T\) and \(p'_i\le p_i\) for all \(i\notin T\) (with \(|T|=k\)), then \(\Pr_{S\sim r_{\mathrm{Samp}}(p')}[S=T]\ge \Pr_{S\sim r_{\mathrm{Samp}}(p)}[S=T]\) (Theorem 1.1). It also proves impossibility results for stronger coalition/threshold monotonicity notions (e.g., no full-support quota+ex-ante proportional method satisfies pairwise threshold monotonicity, Theorem 4.6), and shows Grimmetts method satisfies threshold monotonicity only for coalitions of size \(|T|\le 2\) (Theorem 4.5).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks whether one can have (i) ex ante proportionality, (ii) universal (ex post) population monotonicity in the sense of being a distribution over deterministic population-monotone rules, and additionally (iii) at least one one-sided quota bound (lower or upper) almost surely.

This paper does not construct a method with universal population monotonicity; instead it studies different, coalition-probability-based monotonicity axioms for randomized methods that already satisfy full quota (both lower and upper) ex post. In particular, the Sampford-induced apportionment method satisfies quota and ex-ante proportionality, but the paper does not claim (and its discussion of prior work [17] suggests skepticism about) representability as a mixture over deterministic population-monotone rules. Thus it does not resolve existence/nonexistence for the open problem.

Nevertheless, it is relevant because it identifies Sampford sampling as a particularly well-behaved rounding scheme and develops techniques/axioms for monotonicity beyond expectations, which could inform attempts to reconcile stronger monotonicity requirements with quota-type constraints.

sources.title: New combinatorial insights for monotone apportionment
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper shows that within stationary divisor methods (which are ex-post population-monotone), for every instance 
- there is an interval of parameters where all outcomes satisfy upper quota and an interval where all outcomes satisfy lower quota (Proposition 1),
and it proves strong negative results for randomizing over stationary divisor methods: for any distribution over 
\(\delta\in[0,1]\), there exist instances where some state’s expected seat share deviates from its quota by nearly \(H/2\) (Proposition 4). It also gives a positive construction if one relaxes the fixed-house-size requirement: a randomized fixed-divisor method that is quota-compliant, population-monotone, and ex-ante proportional, but may deviate from house size ex post (Theorem 2).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks whether there exists a randomized method with (i) ex-ante proportionality, (ii) universal/ex-post population monotonicity (i.e., every deterministic rule in the support is population-monotone), and (iii) at least one one-sided quota bound (lower or upper) holding almost surely, all while meeting the house size exactly.

This paper does not construct such a method with fixed house size and one-sided quota almost surely. Instead:
- It analyzes stationary divisor methods (which are universally population-monotone) and proves that for each instance, choosing \(\delta\) near 1 yields lower-quota compliance and choosing \(\delta\) near 0 yields upper-quota compliance (Proposition 1). However, no single fixed \(\delta\)-divisor method satisfies ex-ante proportionality in general, and randomizing over \(\delta\) does not guarantee ex-ante proportionality either.
- It provides a negative result that randomizing over stationary divisor methods cannot get close to ex-ante proportionality uniformly: worst-case expected deviation from quota remains \(\Omega(H)\) (Proposition 4). This suggests that simply mixing population-monotone divisor rules will not solve the open problem.
- It gives a positive method achieving population monotonicity + quota + ex-ante proportionality only after relaxing the requirement that the total seats equal \(H\) ex post (Theorem 2), which is outside the open problem’s model.

So the paper offers partial progress: it clarifies limitations of a natural approach (randomizing over divisor methods) and shows a near-miss positive result under a relaxation, but it does not resolve existence under the original fixed-house-size requirement with one-sided quota a.s.

sources.title: Candidate monotonicity and proportionality for lotteries and non-resolute rules: J. Peters et al.
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper defines Phragmén’s fractional rule (PFR) and proves (Theorem 5) that PFR is candidate monotone, satisfies an ex-ante proportionality axiom (ex-ante PSC), and that its fractional outcome can be decomposed (via dependent rounding) into a lottery over committees each satisfying PSC (an ex-post proportionality axiom). As a consequence for party-list elections (Theorem 6), it yields a randomized apportionment method with ex-ante exact quota (expected seats equal fractional quotas) and ex-post quota (each party’s seats always in \(\{\lfloor q_i\rfloor,\lceil q_i\rceil\}\)) while also satisfying PSC under a party-list-as-ranked-preferences interpretation.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks whether one can have (i) ex-ante proportionality (expected seats equal quotas) and (ii) universal population monotonicity (no population paradox for every deterministic rule in the support), while also enforcing at least one one-sided quota bound (lower or upper quota) almost surely.

This paper is adjacent but does not resolve it:
- It constructs a probabilistic rule for multiwinner voting and, in a party-list special case, obtains ex-ante exact quota and ex-post quota (hence both lower and upper quota simultaneously) (Theorem 6).
- However, the monotonicity it proves is candidate monotonicity (shifting a candidate up in rankings cannot decrease its selection probability), not population monotonicity for apportionment. It does not analyze the population-paradox notion required in the open problem, nor does it show that every deterministic apportionment in the support is population-monotone.
- The dependent-rounding decomposition guarantees ex-post quota/PSC for committees in the support, but gives no guarantee that each support outcome is population-monotone across changing population profiles.

Thus, it provides a construction achieving ex-ante proportionality + (two-sided) quota ex-post in a related setting, but does not establish the key missing ingredient: universal population monotonicity together with one-sided quota for apportionment.

sources.title: Affirmative Action's Cumulative Fractional Assignments
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves (in its own cumulative reservation model) an impossibility and two existence results for randomized rounding under monotonicity and quota-type constraints:
- Theorem 1: No deterministic solution can be monotonic, stay within university quota, and have finite bias.
- Theorem 2: There exists a randomized solution that is monotonic, ex ante proportional, and stays within department quota.
- Remark 2: By assigning the random roster at the university level instead of per-department, one obtains a randomized solution that is monotonic, ex ante proportional, and stays within university quota.
- Theorem 3: The Theorem 2 solution also approximately satisfies university quota with high probability.
- Theorem 4 (single-period, no monotonicity): there exists a randomized solution that is ex ante proportional and stays within both department and university quota.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks whether one can add at least one one-sided quota bound (lower or upper quota almost surely) to a randomized apportionment method that is (i) ex ante proportional and (ii) population-monotone ex post (universal). This paper is not directly about population-monotone apportionment across population profiles; instead it studies multi-period cumulative reservation with a monotonicity constraint (irreversibility over time) and quota constraints at two aggregation levels.

Nevertheless, it is relevant because it explicitly constructs randomized methods that are ex ante proportional and satisfy a strong ex post monotonicity notion (their monotonicity: cumulative allocations never decrease) while also satisfying a quota-type rounding property almost surely at one level (department quota in Theorem 2; university quota via Remark 2). These are analogous to satisfying one-sided (indeed two-sided, floor/ceiling) quota constraints almost surely together with ex ante proportionality and an ex post monotonicity constraint.

However, it does not resolve the specific apportionment open problem: it does not address population monotonicity across different population vectors \,\(p\to p'\) (no population paradox) for each deterministic rule in the support, nor does it prove existence/nonexistence of methods satisfying that property plus one-sided quota in the apportionment setting. So it provides related constructions/techniques and partial conceptual progress, but not a full solution.

sources.title: Random apportionment: A stochastic solution to the Balinski-Young impossibility
sources.theorem_or_result: From the title/abstract only, the paper appears to propose a randomized (“stochastic”) apportionment method intended to circumvent the Balinski–Young impossibility for deterministic methods (e.g., incompatibility of quota, population monotonicity, and other axioms). No precise theorem about almost-sure one-sided quota (lower or upper) together with ex ante proportionality and universal population monotonicity can be confirmed from the provided abstract.
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether one can add an almost-sure one-sided quota guarantee (lower or upper) to a randomized method that is both ex ante proportional and universally population-monotone. A paper titled “Random apportionment: A stochastic solution to the Balinski-Young impossibility” is plausibly addressing exactly this landscape (randomization to evade deterministic impossibilities) and may construct a randomized rule with ex ante proportionality and some monotonicity property. However, with only a truncated abstract and no PDF, it is not possible to verify whether their monotonicity is the strong ex post/universal population monotonicity required here, nor whether they achieve almost-sure lower or upper quota (as opposed to quota in expectation or with high probability).

sources.title: Randomized Apportionment Methods for Exact Proportional Representation: a Short Survey
sources.theorem_or_result:
Based on the available information (title + one-line abstract snippet), the paper appears to be a short survey of randomized apportionment methods achieving exact proportionality in expectation (i.e., 
\(\mathbb{E}[F_i(p,h)]=q_i(p,h)\)). No specific new theorem about simultaneously achieving universal population monotonicity and one-sided quota (lower or upper) can be identified from the provided material.
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether there exists a randomized method that is (i) ex ante proportional, (ii) universally population-monotone ex post, and (iii) additionally satisfies almost-sure lower quota or almost-sure upper quota. A survey on randomized methods for exact proportionality is potentially relevant background because it may catalog known constructions (e.g., randomized rounding, lotteries over deterministic apportionments) and known impossibility/compatibility results among proportionality, monotonicity, and quota. However, with only the title and a truncated abstract, there is no evidence that this survey resolves the existence question or proves an impossibility for adding one-sided quota to population-monotone ex ante proportional methods.
